item.title: Who Can Lead India Toward Sustainable Development?
item.description: India's efforts to expand female representation in government are welcome, and they may well help to advance sustainable development. But how many women hold office matters less than which women do – and whom they represent.

item.title: The Paradox of Democratic Backsliding
item.description: Around the world, authoritarian and populist leaders are eroding civil liberties, weakening democratic checks and balances, and browbeating the press and the judiciary; but rather than taking to the streets in protest, many voters apparently love it. What explains this apparent political masochism?

item.title: Trump’s Unaffordable Midterm Stimulus
item.description: A new report from the Congressional Budget Office shows that the US fiscal outlook has deteriorated sharply since Donald Trump returned to the White house last year. But rather than addressing America's shaky public finances, Trump is courting disaster by devising ways to juice the economy before November's midterm elections.

item.title: Magical Thinking Won’t Make Europe an AI Power
item.description: As long as the European Union remains dependent on a handful of US tech companies, its ambitions to become a global leader in AI will remain out of reach. In today’s geopolitical landscape, strategic autonomy is defined by ownership and control of critical infrastructure.

item.title: How Protectionism Sank American Shipping
item.description: More than a century of shielding domestic shipbuilding and shipping has left the industry less competitive than ever. President Donald Trump’s latest effort to revitalize it risks making matters worse by driving up trade costs and entrenching the very structural weaknesses it purports to fix.

item.title: Reimagining the Economics of Culture
item.description: Standard economic frameworks and metrics for assessing economic output have systematically undervalued the dynamic, long-run returns that culture and the arts generate. Major cultural events like Brazil’s Carnival highlight this failure – and offer a powerful corrective to it.

item.title: Preparing for AI-Enabled Bioweapons
item.description: The prospect of bioweapons developed by non-state actors has lent new urgency to multilateral pandemic preparation and response efforts. Yet at a time when AI has made such threats exponentially greater, the funding model that sustains critical health-security safeguards is collapsing.

item.title: America’s Latest Middle-East Fiasco
item.description: US President Donald Trump did not decide to go to war against Iran because he had run out of diplomatic options, or because an orderly regime change had become realistic, or because military strikes would neutralize any imminent security threat. He launched another US war in the Middle East – toppling a core campaign promise to his MAGA base – to advance his own personal and political interests. Iranians and people throughout the region will pay a high price, but so could Americans if Trump’s lawlessness abroad advances his authoritarian ambitions at home.

item.title: Afghan Women, Too
item.description: The Taliban’s war on women continues to plumb new lows with the enactment of a criminal procedure code that fails to criminalize most forms of gender violence. On International Women’s Day, the world should recognize, commend, and support the organizations inside Afghanistan that continue to stand up for gender equality.

item.title: Why Attack Iran?
item.description: The Trump administration’s propaganda about its foreign policy motivations for starting a regime-change war in Iran strains credulity. Instead, the surrounding evidence implies that the US attack could very well be about undermining American democracy, enriching Donald Trump, or both.

item.title: Saving Innovation From the Anti-Vaxxers
item.description: With a hostile political atmosphere making investors increasingly wary of vaccine-related research in the US, firms are winding down development programs, shelving manufacturing plans, and laying off workers. While these responses are understandable, they are short-sighted.

item.title: The Baby Bump From Remote Work
item.description: A new study across 38 countries found that among adults aged 20-45 who work from home at least one day per week, actual births since 2023 and planned family size are higher. This implies that an increase in remote work would boost fertility much more effectively than expensive pronatalist policies.

item.title: Germany’s Role in a Post-American Europe
item.description: America’s rejection of transatlanticism will dramatically change Europe internally. Whether the continent will persist as an autonomous political entity now depends on Germany's ability to preserve the spirit of cooperation that animated the European project when the United States was still on board.

item.title: How Bombing Iran May Blow Back on America
item.description: The fact that Donald Trump could unleash the immense power of the US military against Iran with brazen disregard for facts and law will only embolden him to use such powers again. Trailing badly in opinion polls, will he use the backdrop of violence in Iran to justify domestic repression in the run-up to the mid-term elections?

item.title: The War on Iran and the War on Anthropic
item.description: US President Donald Trump, who came to power promising no new foreign military entanglements, especially in the Middle East, has now launched a potentially riskier one than the Iraq War a generation ago, and with even flimsier justification. But his use of state power to crush a private company may have even more far-reaching effects.

item.title: Ayatollah Khamenei’s Deadly Legacy
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s authority rested on a mix of religious legitimacy, military loyalty, and economic control, all anchored within constitutional mechanisms that concentrate power while preserving the outward appearance of legality. This authoritarian system will most likely prevent genuine, lasting change in Iran.

item.title: The Perils of a Power Vacuum in Iran
item.description: The US sees regimes it can strike and concludes that striking them resolves the dangers they pose. But eliminating a visible adversary does not neutralize the underlying threat; it merely transforms that threat into something elusive, decentralized, unaccountable, and impossible to negotiate with or monitor.

item.title: Trump’s Risky War of Choice in Iran
item.description: Once again, the US has opted to make a massive strategic commitment in the Middle East. But while it takes only one side to begin a war, it takes two to end it – and Iran now has a vote in how big this conflict becomes and how long it continues.

item.title: British Lessons About American Decline
item.description: Rather than indulging in anti-immigration culture wars and tarring Britain as a case study in "civilizational erasure," Donald Trump and his followers’ would do well to examine how Britain struggled with the loss of its own global preeminence. No other country’s recent past better represents America’s possible future.
